title: Pyramids Of Giza
description:
The most famous of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World have held a deep say over the human imagination for almost two centuries. It also the oldest of those Seven Wonders and the best preserved of them. For almost four thousand years after its construction, the Great Pyramid was the tallest structure in the world. These pyramids are also regarded as the oldest of the Seven Wonders of the World. Ever since their discovery, the pyramids have confounded engineers and scientist as regards to how they were built, their astronomical alignment and the significance of their tombs.
The Pyramids of Giza are one of the seven wonders of the world and it is mandatory to visit them during your trip to Cairo. The best way to explore Adler Planetarium is by booking Pyramids of Giza tickets, well in advance. You can buy the Pyramids of Giza tickets from the ticket counter. Moreover, in order to explore this attraction in much detail, you can also book the Pyramids of Giza tour tickets. If you’re looking for a detailed tour, then you can anytime buy the Pyramids of Giza tour tickets and get some quick facts about the most famous wonder of the world. Also, visit the popular attractions in the city by following Cairo itinerary 4 days.

title: Sphinx
description:
The Terrifying One, as the Great Sphinx is known in Egypt, is built of limstone and has the head of a man with the face of a Pharaoh, a hawk or a ram with the body, and tail and legs of a lion. Modern Egyptology holds that the Sphinx was built in 2500BC by Khafra.There has been much controversy over the true age of the Sphinx, circling around the question whether the erosion of its structure is wind or water caused. If the water erosion hypothesis is true, then the Sphinx could be four thousand years older than originally thought.
Cairo, the land of Sphinx is undoubtedly a world’s wonder for more reasons than one. Being one of the most popular attractions, Sphinx Tickets B are always the talk of the town. description:
Islamic Cairo is a historic area that was created and nurtured by the Muslims after conquering Egypt. The area is beautiful and the atmosphere here is nostalgic. The old woodwork, stonework, and ironwork are dominant in this part of Cairo helping Islamic Cairo subtly maintain its ancient beauty. There are plenty of mosques here that pour peace and tranquility out in the air. There are quite a few cafes and restaurants here too. Also, visit the popular attractions in the city by following Cairo itinerary 4 days.

title: The Egyptian Museum
description:
This Egyptian museum is located at Tahrir square. This museum is peculiarly famous for huge and small statues, jewels, the mummies, Tutankhamon treasures, etc. It includes round about 107 halls which exhibit different antiques from the ancient Egypt culture. The museum also has a section specially dedicated for photography and even comprises of a huge library. Egyptian museum is overall divided into seven sections and you will surely need more than 2 hours to explore all these sections. History lovers would surely find all the ancient coins, papyrus, sarcophagi and scrabs, monuments of different periods very much interesting. There is a hall full of mummies and this is definitely an exciting experience. Along with Egyptians, millions of tourists visit this museum during their stay in Egypt.
Go back in time by visiting the historically significant attraction of Egyptian Museum by getting the tickets for Egyptian Museum under the section of Egyptian Museum tickets. title: The Serapeum Of Saqqara
description:
A serapeum is a temple dedicated to the god Serapis, a combined avatar of Osiris and Apis. Not much is known about this specific serapeum. The construction of this place was dedicated to the sacred bulls of Apis, who they believed were the incarnation of Ptah, the Memphis god. Some eight million dogs are buried here. The serapeum has held a mystic hold over the imaginations of archeologists a long time before it was found.
Storied would filter through to the academia of it’s presence, perhaps a glimpse of a subterranean cavern, a stumbling upon a strange passageway, which was lost again under the sands. For a long time, the site was closed to the public, with officials citing safety hazards. After three decades of intense debates as to the nature of the restoration of the serapeum, reconstruction work started in 2001, taking 10 years to complete and the tomb is now finally open to the public.  Also, visit the popular attractions in the city by following Cairo itinerary 4 days.
tips:
Availability for parking place is doubtful.
Avoid visiting Serapeum after sunset.

title: Mohamed Ali Mosque
description:
Commonly known as Alabaster Mosque, Mohamed Ali Mosque is a holy shrine located in the Citadel in Cairo. This mosque was the largest such structure built during initial years of the 19th century. The interiors of the mosque are indeed impressive. As the mosque is located atop a hill you can enjoy breathtaking views of the pyramid here as well. The architecture of the Mohamed Ali Mosque is somewhat similar to the Blue mosque in Istanbul. Also, visit the popular attractions in the city by following Cairo itinerary 4 days.
Citadel: 40 EGP

title: The Pyramids At Dahshur
description:
The royal necropolis at Dashur is notable for several of the oldest and best preserved pyramids of Egypt, most prominent of which are the Red Pyramid and the Bent Pyramid. The Red Pyramid, which is the only smooth sided pyramid in the world, is the only one accessible to the public.
The Bent Pyramid here is unique in having two separate entrances. The good condition of these pyramids is owed to the hot dry air of the area. Currently the site is under threat of desecration from the unmonitored locals who have moved back into the security vacuum here created by the 2011 Egyption revolution. Also, visit the popular attractions in the city by following Cairo itinerary 4 days.
tips:
Adult: EGP 60
Kids: EGP 30
Parking: EGP 5
address: Monshaat Dahshour, Al Badrashin, Giza, Egypt, Cairo

title: Step Pyramid Of Zoser
description:
The pyramid was built by one of Ancient Egypts most prominent personalities, Vizier Imhotep. It comprises of a series of mastabas (flat-roofed structure with sloping sides), built on top of each other. There are over five kilometers worth of tunnels under the pyramid, built to discourage looting.
Nevertheless, the Pyramid has been well and thoroughly plundered over the years, leaving only the mummified left foot of Djoser. The Step Pyramid of Zoser is unfortunately closed for visitors, but you can surely have a good view of the pyramid from outside and can click hundreds of pictures right in front of it. Also, visit the popular attractions in the city by following Cairo itinerary 4 days.
tips: You will require a personal vehicle or a tour package to access the site.

title: Old City
description:
The old city in Cairo is a true historic dig. The area has spectacular monuments, old churches, mosques, and synagogues. The old city is pretty unique and quite different from the rest of Cairo. There are a total of 7 churches in the area. The religious shines including the mosques and synagogues contribute largely to the peace in the area. If you want to witness the charm of Cairo in an exceptionally different way, the old City is quite a recommended visit. Also, visit the popular attractions in the city by following Cairo itinerary 4 days.

description:
The Pyramids of Cairo account for an incredible experience altogether. Kefre, the second pyramid adds to the same feeling. description:
The Mosque and Madrasa of Sultan Hassan is one of Cairo’s finest examples of the ancient Mamluk architecture in the city. The holy shrine is not only a gorgeous structure but also a peaceful place to spend some time at. The interiors of the mosque are commendable too. The construction of the mosque was completed in two halves due to misfortune and uncertainties. Sultan Hassan who first undertook the building of the mosque was killed about 2 years before the mosque was completed. However, the mosque was finally completed one of his functionaries named Bashir Al Gamdar. Also, visit the popular attractions in the city by following Cairo itinerary 4 days.

It’s famous for not only its Coptic Architecture, but also for being one of the first examples of the Basilican style. The entrance of the church is worth seeing as it is completely decorated and has an open courtyard, which has twenty nine steps leading towards the church. The church has marble pulpit, icons, murals and even inlaid screens. The most famous site of this church is the Virgin Mary which is definitely worth watching. Also, visit the popular attractions in the city by following Cairo itinerary 4 days.

description:
The castle of Saladin which is also known as The Citadel is located on Mokattam hill exactly in the centre of the city. It is one of the most popular places and is famous for its cool fresh breeze and grand views of Cairo. Now, the castle is known to be a preserved site along with its museums and mosques. Saladin castle also includes four other museums namely Al-Gawhara Palace museum, Carriage Museum, Egyptian Military museum and The Egyptian Police Museum. The citadel depicts the political life of Egypt in ancient times and also many important events of history of Egypt. Citadel is also considered to be most famous among the wonders of ancient world. Also, visit the popular attractions in the city by following Cairo itinerary 4 days.

The park serves as a great getaway from the hustle and bustle of the city. You could simply spend an evening here with family and friends enjoy the glorious sunset at the cafe while sipping on a steamy hot cup of tea or coffee.

title: Ben Ezra Synagogue
description:
The synagogue of Ben Ezra is also known as El-Geniza Synagogue or the Synagogue of the Palestinians and is located in the Old Cairo exactly behind the Hanging church. It was originally a Coptic Christian church, sold to the Jews in the ninth century in order to pay the annual tax imposed by the Muslim rulers at the time.
Over the years it became a prime pilgrimage destination, and the most visited Jewish site in Cairo. Many major festival celebrations of Jews take place here. The synagogue is of basilica style and has two floors,one each for men and women.

title: Bab Zuweila
description:
Bab Zuweila is an iconic structure that was built sometime in 11th century. They are actually massive gates flanked by 2 towers. These gates are oldest and the most elegant piece of architecture of the olden period. Bab Zuweila is historically significant and impressive. Make a trip here and dive into the history of Cairo when the city had hardly any commendable fortified structures. A visit to the lovely gates would indeed be informative and interesting.

title: Bazar Khan El Khalili
description:
Bazar Khan El Khalili is located in the Islamic district of Cairo. Tourists and Egyptians alike flock to this place for its countless shopping opportunities. There are several shops in this Bazaar selling everything from basic market fare to unique antiques. Bazaar Khan El Khalili also houses many coffee stores, restaurants and street food vendors. There is also the Al Hussein Mosque in this Bazaar; so those who are religious can surely visit this mosque. The Bazaar is open air, busy and colorful and offers everything right from perfumes, spices, jewelry to souvenirs. Those who are thinking of buying gifts for close ones, friends, family, relatives, can surely shop here and find an extensive collection of Papyrus, Perfume, Scarves, T-shirts and other Textiles, Hand-Blown Glass, Jewelry, etc.
The Bazar Khan El Khalili in Cairo is one of those places where you can shop till you drop, getting all sorts of stuff like antiquities, perfumes, souvenirs and local dishes. Being an open market, there are no tickets for Bazar Khan El Khalili. You can also visit the bazar as a part of a tour or package of other Cairo attractions. You can find and buy tickets for Bazar Khan El Khalili tours on our ticket booking section and check in detail Bazar Khan El Khalili ticket prices too. It is always better to combine Bazar Khan El Khalili tickets with other great tours for some excellent deals.

title: Mosque Of Ibn Tulun
description:
Ibn Tulun mosque is the oldest and largest mosque in Egypt, built in the 9th century with the intent of accommodating an entire army and is also a historic mosque in Cairo. The mosque of Ibn Tulun is completely made up of red building bricks. The mosque is surrounded by three Ziyadahs. The mosque also attracts millions of tourist every year because of its brilliant architecture. The mosque itself is the third largest mosque in the world. The minaret of this famous mosque of Ibn Tulun is a popular landmark of Cairo and is even completely unique in its design.
The oldest and largest mosque in Egypt, the Mosque of Ibn Tulun boasts of a splendid architecture and humongous area. Mosque of Ibn Tulun ticket prices are zero as there is no entry fee. The scintillating church is actually cut into a cave with well-done interiors, stone sculptures cut into walls and a lot more. There are a total of 3 churches here including a hidden chapel. The church is massive and the experience of hearing a mass here is out of the world.

Zamalek is a little island in the Nile River. The gorgeous island features gardens, classy hotels, restaurants and more. The island is located between downtown Cairo and Giza. There are plenty of stores here and the streets are very well lit. If you have a craving for Indian spices, there is an Indian restaurant here too. It’s a true delight to be strolling along the mystic waters of the longest river while on Gezira(Zamalek) Island.

title: Mosque Of Al- Azhar
description:
The iconic structure of the holy mosque was founded in AD 970. The mosque is one of Cairo’s oldest and the sheikh of the mosque is said to be the highest theological authority for Egyptian Muslims. The structure of the mosque comprises of more than one architectural style. There are three minarets in the mosque that stand from north to south. These minarets belong to the 14th, 15th, and 16th centuries. The Mosque of Al-Azhar is indeed a charming structure in the center of the city. It is also a peaceful and tranquil place to spend a few hours at.

This is Cairo’s main street that’s now only for pedestrians. Sharia Al Mu'izz Li-Din Allah is replete with historic sites. It is also home to old cafes, restaurants, madrassa (schools), hospitals, old minarets, hot pools, and mausoleums of the Mamluk, ottoman, and abuyyid eras. The cobbled streets here are ideal for walking and admiring the gorgeousness of ancient architecture and cherishing the marvels of the bygone time. description:
This is a sixteen floor tower which will give you an awesome experience. The tower is one of the main landmarks of the city of Cairo and is also considered to have excellent architecture. It is built in shape of different plant lives of ancient Egypt. Many of the tourists specially visit the Cairo Tower just to have a great panoramic view of the city.
You will get heartwarming views of the Nile, different parts of Cairo, Saladin Citadel, the old campus of the American University, the Abdeen Palace, Qaser El Nile Bridge, Al Azhar Mosque, and even the pyramids. Free telescopes are also offered at the top floor so that visitors can explore Cairo in an amazing way. Visitors will also get all kinds of drinks and snacks in the revolving restaurant of the tower.

The palace was later converted into a museum with an extensive collection of hunting trophies and the prince’s collection of medieval manuscripts, clothing and more. The museum features interesting and impressive exhibits. The palace garden blossoms with rare tropical plants too.

Memphis Museum is one of the most popular museums in Cairo and is also a rare place where the remains of the old city is kept alive. A visit to the museum is a great opportunity to go back in time. description:
The street is filled with a variety of old buildings. The street is replete with old constructions with nostalgic architecture. There are plenty of old fashioned cafes and restaurants here. The historic feature of this street is truly commendable. You would also come across a lot of mosques here. If you wish to see what old Cairo looked like, the Muez El Deen Allah Street is an area you should pay visit to.
Hire a Taxi.

title: Heliopolis
description:
Similar to the old down town Cairo, Heliopolis is home to old quarters the have old architecture and are very well preserved. There is a shopping area here which is next to the beautiful old arcade. Heliopolis is a blend of the old and the new. It features old constructions as well as new ones. There are plenty of shops, cafes and restaurants here too. Area is quite a fun place to while away some time when in Cairo.
Hire a Taxi.

title: Oum El Dounia Gallery
description:
Oum El Dounia translates to "Mother of the world" which is an Arabic nickname given to Egypt. The charming gallery is a myriad of colors and a wide collection of books and handicrafts. The gallery also works with NGO associations. The gallery can also be looked at as a commercial space where you can buy authentic Egyptian handicrafts at reasonable prices.
So buy a souvenir or take home some gorgeous stuff for your interiors, Oum El Dounia Gallery provides with everything that looks artistic.
address: 1er etage, 3 Talaat Harb, Bab Al Louq, Abdeen, Cairo Governorate, Egypt

title: Saint Sergius Church
description:
St. Sergious Church is regarded as one the oldest churches in the city of Cairo. The church is said to be one of the places where the Holy Family escaped and rested. A mass is held here every first June to commemorate the event. The crypt in which they were said to have resided is 10 meters deep, and can often be flooded when the Nile is high. Its dedicated to the soldier-saints martyred during the 4th century in Syria.
Parking options available.
Special facilities offered for person with special needs.
tips:

address: Abu Serga Old Cairo, Cairo, Egypt.

title: Bayt Al- Suhaymi
description:
Bayt Al-Suhaymi is an Islamic house located close to Muiz street in a small alley. The house is famed for its spectacular wooden balconies, classical rooms with Arabic calligraphy and wooden panels. If Islamic architecture and ancient wooden work interests you, the Bayt Al-Suhaymi is quite a recommended visit for you when in Cairo.
The house is impressive and classic. On special occasions, the house hosts music concerts as well.

title: The Pharaonic Village
description:
At Pharaonic village, you will completely experience the ancient Egypt like never before. You can surely have a great vacation experience in this village with various family-friendly activities. The Pharaonic village is a living museum that depicts the ancient Egyptian life. In this Pharaonic village you will see a complete replica of the tomb of King Tutankhamun.
This village is only accessible by boat and it has two cafes, a mock temple and several museums. Toursare conducted in this Pharaonic Village in various languages. The village includes activities like Children's Amusement Park, Nefertari Yacht, Dining, Shopping, Entertainment, Cleopatra's Photo Studio, Art Center, Event Management and Filming.
tips:
It is accessible only by boat.
Tours are conducted in many languages.
address: 3 El Bahr El Aazam St  240 Al Orman, Giza, Egypt, Cairo

title: City Of The Dead
description:
The City of the Dead, also referred to as the northern cemetery, is an area home to a couple of funerary complexes. The city of the dead comprises of the Mosque of Qaitbey which was completed in 1474 AD, Mausoleum of Ibn Barquq, the Complex of Sultan Ashraf Barsbey and more. The irony of this place is that although it is called the city of the dead the place is pretty crowded.
The northern cemetery is basically a historic cemetery of Cairo that is now occupied by those people who couldn’t afford an expensive accommodation in the city.
address: East of Sharia Salah Salem, Cairo, Egypt

title: Cairo International Airport
description:
Cairo International Airport (IATA: CAI, ICAO: HECA) is the international airport of Cairo and the busiest airport in Egypt and serves as the primary hub for EgyptAir, EgyptAir Express and Nile Air as well as several other airlines. The airport is located in Heliopolis, to the northeast of the Cairo around 15 kilometres (9.3 mi) from the area of the city and has an area of approximately 37 square kilometres (14 sq mi). It is the second busiest airport in Africa after OR Tambo International Airport in Johannesburg, South Africa.The airport is operated by the Cairo Airport Authority, which is a subsidiary of the Egyptian Holding Company for Airports and Air Navigation (EHCAAN). The airport serves as a hub for EgyptAir, EgyptAir Express, Nile Air, and several other airlines. It is also the main hub for the Egyptian Air Force.The airport has three terminals: Terminal 1, Terminal 2, and Terminal 3. Terminal 1 is the oldest and serves domestic flights. Terminal 2 is the newest and serves international flights. Terminal 3 is used for charter flights.The airport has two runways: Runway 05/23 and Runway 15/33. The airport also has a cargo terminal, a VIP terminal, and a general aviation terminal.The airport is served by several ground transportation options, including taxis, buses, and shuttles. There is also a train station located at the airport, which connects to the Cairo Metro.Cairo International Airport is the busiest airport in Egypt and the second busiest in Africa. It was established in November 1937, making it one of the oldest airports in the world. The airport was initially used as a military airbase by the British Royal Air Force during World War II.Cairo International Airport is a major hub for air travel in the Middle East and Africa. It is the main hub for EgyptAir, the national airline of Egypt, and serves as a connecting point for many international airlines. The airport is also a major cargo hub, handling over 1.2 million tons of cargo annually.The airport is a major economic driver for the city of Cairo and the country of Egypt. It is estimated that the airport contributes over $2 billion to the Egyptian economy each year. The airport also provides thousands of jobs to the local population and is a major source of foreign currency for the country.

title: The Fish Market
description:
The fish market is a nice place to visit if you are longing for a pocket friendly and delicious dishes. The major attraction of this restaurant is the buffet salad and few other preparations of seafood. The tagengambery and grilled fish with white chilled wine are the best preparations to pacify your taste buds. So, if you visit the fish market with your kids then try the baba ghanoush and fried grouper. It is advisable to come early as it gets crowded in the Friday afternoon. The visitors can choose the fish and it will be prepared in front of them.
Rented car/Taxi
address: El, Regional Ring Rd, Al Maadi, Cairo Governorate, Egypt

title: Baron Empain Palace
description:
Baron Empain Palace is a super cool place to visit with your friends and families in Cairo. It is an important historical site typically known as the Hindu palace. You will be delighted with the beautiful Indian architecture amidst wide green areas. The major attraction of this mansion is the clock at the top of the building. You can always come and take pictures here in the garden area. Baron Empain Palace is best viewed at night when it is lit up completely. So, you should not miss the landmark of Heliopolis which is a mixture of Indian and Roman architecture.
Rented car/Taxi
address: El-Orouba, El-Montaza, Heliopolis, Cairo Governorate, Egypt

title: Wekalet El Ghouri Arts Center
description:
Wekalet El Ghouri Arts Center in Cairo is a wonderful place to pay a visit. It is a place where you can relive history. Visiting this art centre can be a pleasant and good experience in your trip. The dancers wear colourful costumes. The way the dancers move during their performance makes their costume move and create a wave around them. The location of the place is mesmerizing. The extravagant Arabic architecture with great lighting makes it even more attractive. The overall ambience of the place is attractive to tourists. You will get a proper essence of Egyptian culture once you go inside.
More about Cairo
address: 3 Mohamed Abdo Street, Off Al Azhar street, Al Dar Al Ahmar, Cairo Governorate 12411, Egypt

title: Al-azhar Park
description:
If you are wondering about a beautiful place to spend some quality time with your loved ones, then Al-azhar Park is the best option right at the heart of the city. It is one of the 6 best public places in the world and is well-renowned for its beauty. There is a beautiful castle inside the park which attracts the kids. The restaurant inside the park offers food at a pocket-friendly price. The lawn in the garden is a perfect place for photo shoots and other social gatherings. The lake restaurant and the open-air theatre are the best attractions of Al-azhar Park at night.
Rented car/Taxi
address: Salah Salem St, El-Darb El-Ahmar, Cairo Governorate, Egypt

title: Al-hussein Mosque
description:
The Al-Hussein Mosque has all the qualities that can startle the tourists. The appearance of this mosque is charming indeed and you will find its shape charming. The mosque is pretty old and originally, it got established in the year 1154. As one of the most sacred Islamic religious institutions also, this mosque is internationally renowned. In 19th century, the structure got revamped and hence, you can observe strong hints of Gothic Revival architecture in the structure of the building. The mausoleum of the mosque complex is believed to be present here from the original construction period of the mosque. You can visit Al-Hussein Mosque with your loved ones not only to witness history but to offer a prayer as well.
Rented car / taxi
address: 36 Al-Mashhad Al-Husseini، El-Gamaleya, Qism El-Gamaleya, Cairo Governorate, Egypt

title: Wekalet El Ghoury
description:
Traditional art along with Egyptian theatre shows- if this is what you want to see, then Wekalet El Ghoury is the place where you must come with your family. This place is highly recommended among the locals if you want something different at the end of the day. The artists, dancers and the musicians are highly trained to perform the Tanours shows. You will love the Sufi and folk music on the weekends after 7 pm in the Wekalet El Ghoury at an affordable price. The visitors love the colorful and other vibrant performance in this theatre.
Rented car/Taxi
address: 3 Mohamed Abdo Street, Off Al Azhar street, Al Dar Al Ahmar, Cairo Governorate 12411, Egypt

title: Giza Plateau
description:
Desert climate and well-distributed vegetation if this is what you are looking for in Cairo then Giza Plateau is the place you must come. You will find many tombs scattered here and there. It is advisable to hire a guide and know more about the Sphinx and history related to the large structures. You will be delighted to see the Khufu's pyramid in the northern part of the Giza Plateau. It is a hundred feet tall and the space between the feet is about 35 feet. You must also visit the grand Egyptian museum here beside the pyramid of Menkaura.
Rented car/Taxi

title: Citadel El Ainy
description:
If you are looking to get rid of your monotonous schedule and looking for a perfect site in the heart of the city, then Citadel El Ainy is the right place for you. It is a world heritage site and is famous for offering grand views of the city amidst cool and nice breeze. There are 3 beautiful mosques here with Islamic architecture all around the place. The visitors will be delighted to see the collection of royal carriages and other military items in the Egyptian military museum. Citadel El Ainy will not disappoint you with its collections.
Rented car/Taxi

The Nile Ritz Carlton Hotel, Cairo , Commercial Annex
Felfela
description:
Founded by A. Zaghloul in 1959, in the heart of the greater Cairo, Felfela has become one of the most iconic restaurants in Egypt. Felfela is the first restaurant to promote the Egyptian and the Mediterranean cuisine inside and outside of Egypt. With its very authentic decoration, Felfela offers a unique experience and not just a place to eat. Only in our restaurant, you can feel the originality of the typical oriental decoration that symbolizes the traditions of the Egyptian society. Perfection and excellence is our trademark. Do not miss the opportunity to live this unique experience !
Felfela is a renowned restaurant in Cairo, Egypt. It used to be a private retreat of the Felfela owners. The Felfela village was made open to the public in the year 1974. You will find the atmosphere here to be traditional and countryside in nature. Their menu is one of a kind and include many well-known culinary delights. Some of the dishes that you will find at Felfela are not easily available in Cairo, like baked rice tagines and slow cooked lamb shoulder.

title: Mena House Hotel (formerly Mena House Oberoi)
Nilometer
description:
If you are looking for historical information about the local art and paintings, then Nilometer is the place of your interest. This historical landmark was basically built to measure the level of river Nile during the annual flood seasons. You will not find a better place to enjoy the sunset and clicking nice pictures on the bank of the river Nile. It is a nice place to come for a picnic too as there are plenty of trees to save you from the typical Cairo weather alongside two beautiful cafes. There is a beautiful museum in the Nilometer filled with local artefacts.
Rented car/Taxi

Karnak Temple
description:
Situated along the banks of the Nile River, the Karnak Temple is everything  you can imagine. Dedicated to the Egyptian Gods Amun, Mut and Khonsu, the Temple is spread over some 200 acres and has designated sacred areas for each of the three Gods. The huge premises actually plays host to a large collection of small temples and statues rife with symbolism and illusions-legend has it that every ruler of Egypt contributed to the Temple we know it today by building a temple, statue, etc. Right from the statues that greet you at the entrance to the grand 134 columns hypostyle hall, the Karnak Temple will one of the many jaw dropping things you’ll see in Egypt.

title: Valley Of The Kings
description:
Anywhere you look in Egyptology, you’ll come across the concept of afterlife- the journey of the soul from one world to another! That’s one of the reasons they’re mummification process was so exact and precise. The Valley of the Kings is essentially a burial ground. All the kings and pharaohs of Egypt of the New Kingdom period were buried there with all the possessions they could possibly need in their journey to the afterlife. With over 63 tombs, each tomb is different from the others; the murals lining the walls, the embellishments on the actual coffin/casket couldn’t be any more different.
The entrance ticket you buy covers only three tombs, so choose wisely.
If you want to see Tutankhamun’s Tomb, you’ll have to pay for that separately.
The desert sun can be brutal even during the winter. Carry a hat and a bottle of water with you.

title: Temple Of Hatshepsut
description:
Surrounded by steep and rugged limestone cliffs, the Temple of Hatshepsut is named after its creator. Although there have been many queens in Egypt, there have been none like Queen Hatshepsut. While all other monuments bearing her name or statue were defaced, this one Temple has stood the test of time and contains all the aspects of a memorial temple. The Temple is designed over three terraces that rise above each other. The entrance to the Temple is through the court and a series of steps that lead to the first terrace. The preferred time to visit is before the afternoon sun sets in as it can pretty hot as the day progresses.

title: Luxor Temple
description:
Yet another temple dedicated to the Gods Amun, Mut and Khonsu, the Luxor Temple was especially built to celebrate the Opet Festival, which is where all three Gods come together in a reunion. Two pharaohs of the land are said to have contributed the most to the temple’s architecture are Amenhotep III and Ramses II; the collection of the latter’s statues stands testimony to his contribution. In the olden times, there used to a 3km line of Sphinxes that used to run from the Luxor Temple, all the way to the Karnak Temple. If you get a chance, catch the sound and light show held at the Temple after hours!

title: Luxor Museum
description:
When you visit the Luxor Museum, don’t be disappointed by its size; it’s a lot smaller than the museum you’ll see in Cairo, but just as spectacular mind you! Catch the Wall of Akhenaten, which is made up of small blocks similar to those used in the Karnak Temple. It’s displayed as a tableau which is made from recycled rubble. The highlights of the museum can’t be ranked in order of preference or popularity but among the must see exhibits is the statue of Amenhotep III and the retrieved pieces from Tutankhamun’s tomb.

title: Medinet Habu
description:
Also known as the Mortuary Temple of Ramses III in English, this is a second huge temple complex after Karnak, in Egypt. However, information says it is the least visited destination whereas it does deserve attention. The great pharaohs of Egypt were buried in the Valley of Kings but mortuary temples were built in their honor. This memorial temple of Ramses III is one of them.
Highlighted features of the complex – the southeast corner from where you can get a perfect view of the entire complex; the Chapels of the Votaresses; Sacred Lake; Nilometer; the First Pylon; Royal Palace; the Second Pylon; Hypostyle Hall and so on.

title: Tomb Of Tutankhamun
description:
Situated in the Valleys of the Kings, the Tomb of Tutankhamun was first discovered by Howard Carter and Lord Carnavon, in the year 1922. The boy pharaoh Tutankhamun’s mummy is said to be adorned with gold mask. Right now, the mummy is being kept in the Egyptian museum of Cairo. However, the mummy of King Tut, still resides within the tomb wrapped in a linen cloth and preserved in a glass box. Look around the wall to see some incredible colored wall paintings.
King Tut’s Tomb – 40E£
address: Valley of the Kings, Luxor, Egypt

title: Avenue Of Sphinxes
description:
Located between Karnak temple complex and Luxor temple, Avenue of Sphinxes is the site where commercial processions were held. Though the site connects one of the oldest temples of Luxor, still the site belongs to 380 BC. It is around 2.7 kilometer long and has 1,350 sphinxes lining in it sides. The most famous Sphinx among the lot is the Great Sphinx at Giza. It has cat-life feature. The rest of the lot has rams head.
Hire a cab or ask any local to know about public transport .
tips: Hire a cab or ask any local to know about public transport .

title: Deir Al-medina
description:
Creating so many tombs and temples around the city was not an easy job. Huge groups of artisans worked and gave their best effort to build such awe-inspiring historical sites. However, these artisans required a place to settle down. That is how, the Valley of the Artisans or Deir al-Medina (as known in Arabic) was discovered. When visiting the attraction, there are innumerable features to look forward to.
The Tomb of Inherka that belonged to a 19th dynasty servant who worked in the Place of Truth, the Valley of the Kings. This one room tomb has a splendid wall painting. Next to it is the Tomb of Sennedjem (a 2 small chambered) and the temple of Pashedu are other important features of the valley.

Tombs Of The Nobles
description:
Scattered on the rocky hills on the West Bank of Luxor, the valley of the Nobles is located. The term noble was given to the as it was the burial ground of foremen, priests, soldiers, officials and princes. On this valley, you will get to see at least 400 tombs, divided into groups. Visit each group and you will get to see such beautifully decorated frescoes depicting the lifestyle of nobles. However, presently only few are open to public. Some of the popular ones among the lot are the tombs of Sennofer and the mayor of Thebes (modern-day Luxor).
Each group of tombs within the valley has separate admission fee
Sheikh Abd El-Gurna is a must visit attraction

title: Ramesseum Or Mortuary Temple Of Ramses I I
description:
One of the largest and best preserved temples of Luxor, this million year old mansion is located in the Theban necropolis in Upper Egypt, across the River Nile. History says the temple belonged to the great warrior king Ramesses II. A tour into the temple will let you get a glimpse of the fallen colossus that inspired Bysshe Shelleys to write the poem, Ozymandias. The first and second pylon depicts battle scenes from the Battle of Qadesh. Behind the pylon, the ruins of the huge court remain. The complex also includes the palace of the king, store rooms and granaries, built by mud brick.
The temple is open everyday.

title: Karnak Open Air Museum
description:
Located in the extreme north-west corner of the Precinct of Amon-Re, within Karnak temple complex, this open air museum is situated. A tour within the entire museum will let you see a reconstructed structure of the already demolished dynasty. There are collection of stones, statues and shrines which you cannot give a miss. Other attractions include the White Chapel of Sesostris I, the Red Chapel of Hatsheput and the Alabaster Chapel of Amenhotep I.
It is best to visit the place during late afternoon.
tips: It is best to visit the place during late afternoon.

title: Temple Of Montu Enclosure
description:
Karnark Temple complex has four main temple enclosures and temple of Montu enclosure is one of them. History says, the temple is an Egyptian temple, dedicated to the worship of Monthu. The total area covered is said to be around 20,000 square meters.
Main attractions of the enclosure – the Temple of Montu, Temple of Harpre, Temple of Ma’at, a sacred lake and the Gateway of Ptolemy.
tips:
Try to visit the place in the late afternoon.
You don’t need to spend an extra day to visit this enclosure.

title: Abu Haggag Mosque
description:
Above the ruins of Luxor temple, nestled within the walls of Ramesses II court, stands Abu Haggag Mosque (Mosque of Abu Haggag) and is considered as the ancient center of worship during the reign of Pharaoh Amenhotep III. If you view the mosque from the tops of the architraves in the Colonnade Hall, it dominates the first court at Luxor temple. It is best to visit the mosque during any of the religious festivals of Muslim saints. Boats are paraded around the temple. You could hear the echo of the age old procession of the barks of Amun, Mut and Khonsu, especially during the Festival of Opet.
The mosque is open for the whole day, except during prayer times.
Women should wear a scarf.
tips:

title: Colossi Of Memnon
description:
You know you’re in the West Bank area of Luxor when two statues, 60 ft high greet you! These two statues, or  the Colossi of Memnon as they’re called, were supposedly standing guard to the temple of Amenhotep III. In fact, it would have been the biggest temple complex had the Nile not washed it all down. Now it stands as a landmark on the side on the road, welcoming you to the West Bank.
Anyone who is familiar with Egypt would definitely know of this place. A popular landmark for visitors, the Colossi of Memnon are two manganous statues bespeaking of its now jaded glory and peerless past. Standing amidst the dry plains of desert, it can be visited anytime without any Colossi of Memnon Tickets or Colossi of Memnon Ticket Prices. Which, once again saves you a lot of effort with no lines to Buy Tickets for Colossi of Memnon or fretting over its Colossi of Memnon Ticket Costs. If you are one of those who are more inclined towards relics, the place with its rich history will charm you to the bits.

title: Valley Of Queens
description:
Like the kings of the land, the leading ladies of Egypt too had their own burial ground, just as if not more spectacular than their male counterparts. Around 75 Queens of the 19th and 20th dynasties were buried here for their peaceful transition to the afterlife. Not all of them are open to tourists. In fact, till date only three tombs have been open to tourists, the tickets to which were sold faster than hot cakes. The notable burials here are of course the tombs of Nefertari, Amunherkhepshef, Khaemwaset and Titi.

title: Temple Of Seti I
description:
Set a little outside of the city, the Temple of Seti  is named in honour  of the ruler who started the construction of the temple but did not finish it. Not as frequented by tourists as the other temples, this temple is a little depleted and is used as a quarry by the local people. The walls of the ruined hypostyle hall still have retained some of their carvings showing both Ramses II and Seti I with the gods.

description:
The deity associated with mummification, Anubis, greets you right at the entrance, kicking off the visit to a perfect authentic start. True to its name, the mummification museum displays mummies and even animals of the 21st dynasty. In addition to the many mummy displays, there’s also the paraphernalia used in the process of mummification.
The Mummification Museum in Luxor provides an insight into the world of mummification and how it was practiced in ancient Egypt. You can check out the devices used for the ceremonies and also various mummies kept on display. Mummification Museum ticket prices are provided on our ticketing portal along with combo offers on tickets for Mummification Museum Tours. title: Tomb Of Pabasa
description:
If you are visiting Hatsheput’s temple, make it a point to visit this tomb. It is located just at the entrance of the Deir al-Bahri. History says, Pabasa (also known as Pabes) was the Chief Steward of the God’s Wife Nitocris. The walls of the tomb have drawings depicting wonderful scenes of agriculture, hunting and fishing style of those ages. The walls of the court Pabasa have scenes and long texts with painted hieroglyphs.
The entry  ticket is available at the ticket office of the Deir al-Bahri temple.
It is best to visit the place in late afternoon if you wish to avoid scorching heat of the sun.

title: Dandarah And The Temple Of Hathor
description:
One of the ancient glories, still existing in pieces is Dendera. Located on the western shore of Nile, the primary attraction of this historical site is the Temple of Hathor. A closure look to the architectural design shows that the style is being followed of contemporary period with the life of Christ. One of the fascinating depictions on the walls is the scenes showing Cleopatra VII and her son, fathered by Julius Caesar.
The journey from the city to the place will take an hour.
tips: The journey from the city to the place will take an hour.

title: Dra Abu'l Naga
description:
While visiting Deir al-Bahri, you can see this necropolis, hidden in the dessert cliffs. Dra Abu’l Naga is said to have 100 tombs of rulers and nobles, dating back to 17th dynasty to later period (around 500 BC). However, the entire area had been wrongly spoiled. Still tombs escaped with its remarkable paintings. One of them is the tomb of Roy (No 234) that has scenes depicting funerary offerings and agriculture. Don’t miss out the paintings on the ceiling as well.
Carry hat to protect yourself from the sun.
Wear light clothes to be comfortable.

title: Luxor International Airport
description:
Luxor International Airport (LXR) is an international airport located in Luxor, Egypt. It is the second busiest airport in Egypt after Cairo International Airport. The airport is located 4 km (2.5 mi) east of the city center.The airport was opened in 1970 and is the main gateway to the ancient city of Luxor. It is served by a number of airlines, including EgyptAir, Air Cairo, and Royal Jordanian. The airport is also used for charter flights to and from Europe, the Middle East, and North Africa.The airport has two terminals, one for domestic flights and one for international flights. It has a single runway and is capable of handling up to 2.5 million passengers per year.The airport has a long history, dating back to the time of the Pharaohs. It was used by the ancient Egyptians to transport goods and people to and from Luxor. During the Roman period, the airport was used to transport troops and supplies to the city. In the 19th century, the airport was used by the British to transport troops and supplies to the city during the British occupation of Egypt.Today, Luxor International Airport is an important hub for tourism in Egypt. It is the gateway to the ancient city of Luxor, which is home to some of the most famous monuments in the world, such as the Valley of the Kings, the Temple of Karnak, and the Luxor Temple. The airport is also a major hub for business travelers, as it is located close to the city's business district.Luxor International Airport is located in the city of Luxor, Egypt. It is the second busiest airport in Egypt after Cairo International Airport. The airport was originally built in the 1930s as a military airbase and was later converted into a civilian airport in the 1950s.The airport is of great historical significance as it is located near the ancient city of Thebes, which was the capital of the ancient Egyptian Empire. The airport is also located near the Valley of the Kings, the site of the tombs of many of the Pharaohs of ancient Egypt.The airport is also significant for its role in the development of tourism in Egypt. Luxor is one of the most popular tourist destinations in Egypt, and the airport serves as a gateway for tourists visiting the city. The airport is also a hub for domestic flights to other cities in Egypt, such as Cairo and Aswan.The airport is also significant for its role in the development of the Egyptian economy. The airport is a major hub for cargo flights, and it is also a major hub for international flights. title: Mortuary Temple Of Hatshepsut
description: One of the many wonders in Egypt includes the magnificent Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut in Luxor’s Valley of the Kings. Dedicated to the second female Pharaoh, Queen Hatshepsut, the three-storied temple is cast in sand, and is considered one of the most beautiful royal temples in the region that you must visit. In the reconstructed temple, you can take the long ramps to access the various levels which are open to tourists. There are several statues and ancient painted reliefs of the Queen Hatshepsut, Egyptian Gods and sphinxes that you will come across in the Temple premises. The stark barrenness of the surrounding areas and of the Temple itself is awe-inspiring and will have you transported to an earlier era.

title: Sharm El Luli
description:
Translated as “Paradise” in English, the Sharm El Luli is a sandy beach and lagoon that falls 60 km south of Marsa Alam. It is a long stretch of shimmering beach shore with few rocky outcrops and some coral reefs. On some days, when the tide is low you can easily wade through the waters like you do in a lagoon. In the backdrop of its waters is the mountainous region that looks stunning. Endowed with crystal clear waters, this beach is a popular destination in Marsa Alam thanks to its calm seascape.
If you are not a very strong swimmer it's probably best to use a life jacket when snorkelling to minimize any possible risk.
Avoid going to Sharm El Luli on windy days: The sea will be choppy, underwater visibility will be poor, there will be a lot of sand in the air and it will be almost impossible to enjoy the beach.

title: Elphinstone Reef
description:
Every diver enthusiast would know Elphinstone Reef as one of the most exciting places for diving in the world. This reef is replete with gentle, vibrant corals and fish species along with the terrifying ones such as hungry barracuda, dolphins and sharks.  Here, the divers can swim with some of the most dangerous yet fascinating of sea fishes. Although this place is more for expert divers, there is still something for amateur ones to learn and see. Elphinstone reef is deep, dangerous yet alluring with caves embedded in it. The most interesting part about this reef is that it is shaped like a cigar making divers spellbound to it.
If you want to dive at Elphinstone you must had an Advanced Open Water Diver certificate and you should have experience of at least 50 dives and also experience of deep diving, drift diving and rough sea conditions.  
Your guide should give you a comprehensive briefing and you should know what to do in the event of anything unexpected and also consider what to do if the current is stronger than anticipated.

title: Marsa Mubarak
description:
Marsa Mubarak is the largest bay that falls in the south of Port Ghalib in Marsa Alam. This place is famous for nestling various diving and snorkelling opportunities for which people get attracted to it. What makes this place more attractive is that it receives frequent visits by the green sea turtles and a dugong. Some of its famous diving sites are: North Garden, North Wall, Inner Pinnacles, Simply Seagrass and South Garden. Any visit to Marsa Alam remains incomplete without having visited Marsa Mubarak. You can also spot the elusive Dugong here.
Recommended to be visited during early hours to avoid rush.
tips: Recommended to be visited during early hours to avoid rush.

title: Sataya Reef
description:
Better known as Dolphin Reef to the international tourists, Sataya is situated at the south-eastern tip of the Fury Shoal diving area off Marsa Alam. It boasts of an interesting shape of a horseshoe and is famous for spotting friendly mammals. If you decide to dive here, you will be able to encounter whitetips, fusiliers, Spanish dancers and parrotfish, at different depths, from 4 to 40 metres. Adventurous tourists come here for snorkelling, diving and watching varied sea animals. Take a boat from Marsa Alam to reach here!
You should take sun cream, an underwater camera and binoculars for spotting any surfacing dolphins.  
Also carry - Swimwear, a towel and also your passport (or a photocopy) in case Egyptian police wish to check it on your return the Marsa Alam anchorage.
tips:

title: Port Ghalib
description:
Marsa Alam has recently experienced the surge in tourism owing to many factors such as the opening of an International Airport recently in 2003. Port Ghalib is a newly developed Marina area that houses plush hotels, happening restaurants and many bars to spend leisure time in Marsa Alam. Here the water sports enthusiasts can try on a range of activities such as diving, looking for dolphins, snorkelling, as well as the traditional camel rides and desert safaris. For snorkelers, there are coral reefs that offer mind blowing marine life experiences, including giant Green Turtles. This whole area has been set up in a Moorish style and looks stunning especially at night.
Entry is free.

title: Wadi Gemal
description:
Translated as the Valley of Camels, the Wadi Gemal is a huge area of land with coastal area that lies to the south of Marsa Alam.  Inside this gigantic park, there are varied ecological habitats that nestle variety of animal and bird types including several endangered species. It spans a stretch of mangrove rich coast line and an extensive mountainous area inland which surround one of Egypt's largest desert wadis. You can also stay here for overnight if you wish to have a close rendezvous with its varied landscape. Historically speaking, this area is said to have well known to the pharaohs who based their great wealth on the treasure mined in these mountains.
Recommended to be visited during day time.
tips: Recommended to be visited during day time.
address: Qesm Marsa Alam, Red Sea Governorate, Egypt

title: Marsa Shoona Bay
description:
Marsa Shoona bay is one of the picturesque beaches that adorn Marsa Alam. Here, apart from spending some leisure time, you can also watch turtles passing by. People who love isolation like to visit this place as it remains solitary and aloof from the crowd. This bay area harbours a beautiful reef area full of colourful fishes such as pipefish, scorpions, coral gobies, crocodile fish, clownfish, lionfish and morays, Lounges, umbrellas and bathrooms are available on the beach.
If you are not a very strong swimmer it's probably best to use a life jacket when snorkelling to minimize any possible risk.
tips: If you are not a very strong swimmer it's probably best to use a life jacket when snorkelling to minimize any possible risk.

title: Gebel El Rosas
description:
Also called as the Oriental Bay after the Oriental Bay Hotel here, this area looks picturesque enough to catch your attention. This is a fringing reef that has a sandy offering safe water activities opportunities. This reef is located 18 km north of the city Marsa Alam and is known for its underwater fauna. While diving, you will come across a number of caves and caverns along with a great coral landscape and a lot of anemones. This place is safe to snorkel even during strong winds.
You should also, if possible, take sun cream, an underwater camera (if you haven't got one you might be able to buy a disposable one) and binoculars ( if you have any ) for spotting any surfacing dolphins.

Located inside the famous Iberotel Coraya Beach Resort in Marsa Alam, the Aqua Coraya is a beautiful and fun filled water park that offers immense entertainment to people who visit it. Recently opened in 2009, this park offers wonderful, healthy fun for all age-groups, with slides up to 10 meters height. What makes it even more important to visit is that it is the only Aqua Park near to Marsa Alam in the coast line. Do visit this attraction for ultimate fun and relaxation.
Entry is free.
address: Al Qusair - Marsa Allam Rd, Qesm Al Qoseir, Red Sea Governate, Egypt, Marsa Alam

title: Astronomy Center
description:
There is no other way to appreciate the beauty of any desert than to stargaze. At the astronomy centre in Marsa Alam, the astronomers help you to understand the mystery of the Universe. Equipped with special techniques such as Astro Laser, this attraction tells you the intricacies and concepts of universe in the best way possible. With the help of wide screens, you can see the images with full force.
Foreigners: 20 Euros

title: Intercontinental The Palace Port Ghalib Resort
description:
Located at the famous area of Port Ghalib, the Six Senses Spa is a treat to the tired self. This spa is a popular destination for people who are looking for a unique and enriching wellness experience to balance the senses and thereby ensuring a positive impact on their well-being. This spa combines Asian and Arabic treatments to relax and rejuvenate your body as well as soul. There are couple’s rooms, two Thai treatment rooms, and one wet treatment room with classically designed stone beds. Do visit this spa after your visit to all the attractions in Marsa Alam.

White Desert
description:
The White Desert is, like the name betrays, a desert in Egypt which has multitudes of rock formations that rise up to the skies from the white surface of the desert floor. One can have some great fun, imagining weird and interesting shapes in the rock formations of the desert. The sunlight also changes the color of the dunes and plays quite a few tricks on the untrained eyes. The best way to enjoy the desert is either to visit it during the daytime and witness all the rock formations or just camp it out like a champ under the Egyptian sky, accompanied by Bedouin food and music.
Remember to stay hydrated and carry water bottles with you.
Apply sunscreen and carry head shade with you for the trip.

title: Valley Of Agabat
description:
The Valley of Agabat is a stunning, unearthly work of art, handcrafted by Mother Nature herself and unlike any other petty desert you might have seen earlier. In the valley a vast panorama of sand dunes, soft golden sand and a spectacular view await you as soon as you descend into it. You can find very fine sand on which you can surf, and the occasional seashell as the area used to be under the sea sometime in the ancient past.
Remember to stay hydrated and carry water bottles with you.
Apply sunscreen and carry head shade with you for the trip.

title: Badr's Museum
description:
It is hard to imagine a place like Badr’s Museum in Farafra, but like an oasis in the desert, the museum too attracts visitors to itself, for the marvelous pieces of art that have been built here by him over the years. The artist believes in expressing his innermost desires and feelings through his art, and he is able to do it so spectacularly. You will feel moved to another dimension once you take a step inside the museum and can’t help but feel overawed by the humility and ingenuity of the artist who himself will sometimes act as your guide. Apart from the desert, the museum itself is also worth a stop.
Closing time may vary in off-season, so call ahead of scheduling visit.
tips: Closing time may vary in off-season, so call ahead of scheduling visit.

title: Professional Health & Safety Bureau - El Farafra
Abu Simbel Temples
description:
This is far, far removed from the rest of the attractions around Aswan and that lends a bit of a mystic touch to the place.
These temples are massive the biggest in Aswan. You might have seen a bit of them in the movie “The Mummy Returns” the temple with the four seated figures (they’re all Ramesses II). Built in the 13th century BC, the temples were erected by Ramesses II to impress the people south of Egypt  (this probably explains why its so far removed). A mere six centuries later, the temples had fallen into disuse and were well on their way to being submerged by sand. They were only rediscovered in the 19th century.
tips:
Please have read about the temple and all that you’ll see before hand.
If you stay overnight, you can see the sound and light show.
You can stay over night at Abu Simbel.
Carry water.
Do not swim in Lake Nasser. Crocs.

title: Temple Of Isis At Philae
description:
Philae is actually the island that the temple USED to reside on. The construction of the Aswan Dam led to the island’s flooding, so the UNESCO had the whole complex relocated to the Agilkia Island nearby.
The temple here is dedicated to Isis, the goddess of motherhood, magic and fertility, and to her love for Osiris. Compared to most Egyptian god and goddesses, Philae was actually being worshipped quite recently upto to the 6th century A.D! And no wonder during the height of Roman power, she was the greatest of Egyptian gods, her influence reaching all the way to Britain. During the reign of Emperor Justinian, the temple was used as a church. Over the centuries, Early Christians (who carved crosses into the walls) and Muslims damaged much of the temple.
tips:
A visit here can be combined with a visit to the Highdam and the Unfinished Obelisk.
It’s best to take a guided tour the locals are incredibly well informed about the place and will narrate the legends of Osiris and Isis to you.
Wait for other passengers to show up so you all can share the ride.
The guards will photobomb you.
Tickets to the temple are located at the entrance to the pier before the boat ride. You will have to buy the temple ticket before you can haggle with boatsmen. Boats men will attempt to charge about LE 50 per person. Bargain hard and dribve the price down to LE 40 at least.

title: High Dam
description:
The area of the dam is pretty arid and beautiful. Located across the Nile river, the dam controls the flow of the river. The high dam, commonly known as the Aswan dam holds great importance as if it wasn't for the dam, the Nile river would be flooded throughout the year. A glimpse of the dam is bound to urge you to take a boat ride in Lake Nasser, which however wouldn't be a bad idea.
Do not swim in lake Nasser. 
Tary visiting the place during the day.
tips:
Do not swim in lake Nasser. 
Tary visiting the place during the day.

title: Unfinished Obelisk And Fatimid Cemetery
description:
So it’s a big rock project that no one finished. Big deal. It really is a big deal; this could have been the largest ancient obelisk in the world if it had been finished. It would have stood 42 metres tall. The carvers were cutting it right out of the bedrock but abandoned their work when the stone cracked. It’s also famous because its half-finished status lends archaeologistssignificant clues into the workmanship of the ancients. Stairs leading down into the pit of the quarry will reveal some intriguing ancient pictographs.
Right across the road from the quarry, to the west of the Obelisk lies an immense cemetery. The graves belong to local rulers and landlords from around the 9th century. The state of the place is so deteriorated that it’s hard to make out who were buried. Still the mud brick, horned-corner domes are quite a sight. The cemetery has been fenced off, so you’ll have to walk around it to find the main entrance if you want to see anything.

title: Nubian Museum
description:
The museum was brought about when UNESCO appealed to Egyptologists and archaeologists the world over to save Nubia’s relics from the rising waters of the dam-lake Lake Nasser. 60 expeditions rescued thousands of objects from hundreds of sites. The museum housing these relics opened 12 years later on a beautiful waterfall graded landscape.
7000 square metres of museum building and 43000 square metres of gardens and public spaces that’s the space dedicated to Nubian heritage here. The permanent indoor collections have about 1500 items. Cleverly arranged in a chronological manner, theyshowcase Numibian history from prehistory to modern times and cover pottery, statuary, sculptures, metalwork, jewellery, basketry, textiles and cooking implements. The outer grounds are landscaped with some nice garden paths to walk, Islamic buildings and relics to the North, an artificial prehistoric cave to the south (but with real prehistoric paintings), and open air theatre and a model Numibian dwelling that’s true to culture.
address: Nubia Museum, el Fanadek Street (Opposite Basma Hotel), 81111, Aswan, Egypt

title: Elephantine Island
description:
This is the oldest inhabited area of the region, deicated once upin a time to the ram-headed god Khnum. The northern end of the island is taken up by the Moevenpick Resort. The rest of the island offers two Nubian Villages, Aswan Museum and some ruins.
All ticket prices given are for the Museum.
Bring small gifts to give to children and people you interact with – candy and stationery are perfect.
You cannot access the restaurants overland from the south end of the islands. You’ll have to have a boat take you there.
tips:
Nubian Village: Siou and Koti are the two villages on this island. The villagers are quite aware they are part of a tourist attraction and will go out of their way to make you feel hospitable. The shops here are pretty commonplace; you’ll find them even in the town, but the true sights here are the living traditions of the Nubian people. It’s colorful and unique and you should not be surprised to see baby crocs running about. They are kept as pets upto a certain age. If your boat pilot has a house on the island, he will typically invite you in for mead; accept. Tip them. They might even call you into a school to learn a bit of the local lingo.
Aswan Museum: Features Nubian artefacts saved during the construction of the Aswan Dam. Opened in 1912, the museum underwent extensions in the 1990’s, and now houses utensils, pottery, weapons and mummies. South of the museum you’ll fing the ruins of Abu, an ancient city on which excavations are still happening. Best thing to see at the runes is Nilometer, and ingenious ancient device to measure the river water levels during the flood season. It was a supremely important tool. The Nilometer here, at the border of Ancient Egypt was the place in the Empire where the annual flooding was first detected every year.

title: Aswan Botanical Garden
description:
There was literally nothing to do on this island until the Ministry of Irrigation took one look at it and said “Hey, let’s build a Botanical Garden. Every place has one these days”. Even if that’s not exactly how the conversation might have gone down, this place is still a pretty neat achievement. The whole island is the botanical garden now, and really pretty for it, with beautiful pathways shaded by trees. Hundred of species of plants were brought in from the Far East, India and all over Africa. It’s completely populated with birds like hupupa, doves, crows, ravens, sparrow owls, Indian and white peacocks, ducks. A small zoo even has monkeys. At sunset, this place is sheer fairytale beauty.
Don’t come on Friday. Really noisy and really crowded.
tips: Don’t come on Friday. Really noisy and really crowded.

title: Sound And Light Show - Philaetemple Of Kom Ombo
description:
The famous Sound and Light Show at the Philae temple narrates the story of the legends of Goddess Isis & God Osiris. The narration digs deeper divulging interesting historical facts of the temple back in the days when the Philae temple remained flooded for about half a year and the reestablishment of the temple in 1960.  During the restoration process, each stone of Philae temple was relocated.
The temple is open daily.

title: Monastery Of St. Simeon
description:
The monastery here was built on the ruins of an older one and dedicated to St. Simeon. For four centuries the monks here practiced converting Nubians to Christianity till Saladin destroyed the place. The complex has an upper and a lower level. It was once surrounded by a 10 metre high wall, a feature that allowed it to function as a fortress. There are cells here where monks and pilgrims slept; a basilica with faint frescoes. If you really want to know what the highlights are the best way to go about the is by first knowing what you’re going to want to see, then allowing the on-site ticket collector to take you around if he’s okay with it (make sure to tip him). Check out the upper terrace, the refectory, the cell with stone beds, the corridor in the keep, the scenes of Christ, the painting of the Virgin Mary and the painting of the 24 elders of the Revelation.
Carry water.
Wear a hat and sunscreen.
Take a guide book on the complex so you understand what you’re seeing.
tips:
Carry water.
Wear a hat and sunscreen.
Take a guide book on the complex so you understand what you’re seeing.

title: New Kalabsha
description:
Yet another site brought into being by the rising waters of Lake Nasser. The temples and relics here were all relocated from their previous premised that have now submerged.
These include:
tips:
The Kalabsha Temple: Erected by Augustus Caesar himself in dedication to Isis, Osiris and Mandulis. This had been a boon of sorts. Only three rooms are completely decorated, and the half finished work had provided rich information on ancient carving and building techniques. Also to see in the temple complex are a birthhouse, a chapel and a large rock stela.
Beit el-Wali: a rock cut temple dedicated to Ramesses II and the gods Amun and Anukis. It’s brightly coloured decorations look as fresh as ever though apportion of them have been taken to the British Museum. There are various scenes depicting the defeat of enemies by the Pharaoh.
Gerf Hussein: The free standing portion of the temple dedicated to Ramesses II is shown here. The rock cut portion is submerged.
Kiosk of Qertassi: A tiny and elegant Roman kiosk with columns made of brown sandstone. It may have been connected to a temple on the East Bank upto 1813 and may also have been a way station on a processional route.
address: New Kalabsha, Near Aswan, Aswan, Egypt

title: Tombs Of The Nobles And Kubbet Al-hawa
description:
This place just isn’t going to impress you until you know exactly WHO’s buried here. All I’ll say is that they were important… like, really important. Only six of the Old and Middle Kingdom tombs are open to the public, but they are pretty well preserved. The nobles buried here were rulers and dignitaries of Elephantine Island once upon a time. The important tombs are:
Look up these names and see the incredible story behind them if you want to know why this place is awesome.
tips:
Mekhu (Tomb No25) and Sabni (Tomb No 26) governors during the reign of Pharaoh Pepi II (2278–2184 BC). Lovely reliefs and scenes.
Sarenput II (Tomb No 31): Governor during under 12th-dynasty Pharaoh Amenemhat II (1922–1878 BC). Beautifully preserved.
Harkhuf (Tomb No 34): Governer while Pepi II was a kid. Relief tells of his incredible central Africa trading expedition.
Hekaib (Tomb No. 35): Overseer of foreign soldiers under Pepi II. Fighitng and hunting scenes.
Sarenput I (Tomb No 36): Nothing too special, but read his story and see scenes of his family.
address: Tombs of the Nobles, West Bank, Aswan, Egypt

title: Sehel Island
description:
Located about 4 kilometers south in the city of Aswan, the Sehel Island takes a place in the Nile to the north of the old Aswan dam. A visit to this island is an escape to peace. The scenic view and rock inscriptions dedicated to the goddess Anukis and her consort Khnum are sure to catch you by surprise.  The island will give you a nostalgic feeling, reminding you of the time when technology was only a word.
The island can be easily accessed by a local ferry boat.
tips: The island can be easily accessed by a local ferry boat.
address: Gharb Seheil | River Nile, Aswan, Egypt

title: Ferial Gardens
description:
Corniche El Nil is a pleasant road on the east bank and it’s very southern end leads you to a pretty little garden. Though nothing stands out as such, it’s a nice place to stroll about in. Visit during the evening twilight to see couples walking about; yep, at least once place in this town provides some people watching. The garden was brought about to celebrate the birth if Princess Ferial in 1938. As such, it’s a befittingly romantic place.
Entry is free.
address: Ferial Garden, Kornish el-Nil, Aswan, Egypt

title: Aswan International Airport
description:
Aswan International Airport (IATA: ASW, ICAO: HESN) is an airport located in Aswan, Egypt. It is the second largest airport in Upper Egypt after Luxor International Airport. The airport is located about 8 km (5 mi) from the city center.The airport was opened in the 1950s and was initially used as a military airbase. In the 1970s, the airport was upgraded to an international airport and began to serve commercial flights. The airport has one runway and one terminal building.The airport serves as a hub for domestic flights to Cairo, Hurghada, Luxor, and Sharm El Sheikh. It also serves international flights to Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates.The airport is of historical significance as it was the site of the first international air show in Egypt in the 1950s. The air show was attended by the then-President of Egypt, Gamal Abdel Nasser. The airport also served as a base for the Egyptian Air Force during the 1973 Yom Kippur War.Aswan International Airport is located in Aswan, Egypt and is the main airport serving the city. The airport was first opened in the 1950s and was initially used as a military airbase. In the 1960s, the airport was expanded to accommodate commercial flights and was officially opened as Aswan International Airport in 1965.The airport has played an important role in the history of Egypt, as it was used as a base for the Egyptian Air Force during the Six-Day War in 1967. It was also used as a staging point for the evacuation of foreign nationals during the 1973 Yom Kippur War.Today, Aswan International Airport is an important hub for domestic and international flights, connecting the city of Aswan to other major cities in Egypt and the Middle East. The airport is also a popular destination for tourists, as it is located close to many of the city's attractions, including the Aswan High Dam and the Temple of Isis.

title: Aswan Sculpture Park
description:
Every spring, sculptures from around the world come down to Egypt for the International Sculpture Symposium and set about crafting incredible art at the Basma Hotel. From there, their creations are brought to ths park for the world to see and appreciate. The exhibition is set in an ancient grantire quarry over looking a lake. The pieces themselves range from the amusing to the bizarre. From the park itself, two steep staircases lead down to the road, though those with mobility problems are discouraged from attempting this; it would be better to go back the way they arrived. Still, going down the stairs offers great views of the landscape.
Ticket Price: Free.
Timings: Tips:a.k.a Symposium Museum. Very few locals know it as Sculpture Park, so ask for the Sculpture Museum or the Symposium Museum.
Tip: the site hasn’t officially opened yet, so it’s still free to visit. A car to here from the town ought to only cost you LE4.
Carry water.
Wear a hat and sunscreen.
